topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on the screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the quiet monotony of my afternoon. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that tasted like dust. It was the listing. My childhood house. A sterile photograph of the peeling paint and the overgrown garden, now overlaid with a price tag that made my stomach clench. I stared at the screen, my knuckles white where I gripped the cheap plastic remote. A slow, hot pressure began to build behind my eyes. I pushed the phone away, the silence of the apartment suddenly deafening, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse. Why now? Why this intrusion? I stood up, pacing the small living room like a caged animal, running a hand over my face, feeling the familiar, tight knot of resentment tighten in my chest. It felt like a physical weight, heavy and suffocating.

[story 2]

I clicked the link again, my fingers trembling slightly. The details scrolled by: square footage, outdated renovations, the exact layout of the rooms I’d spent a thousand summers inhabiting. A wave of nausea washed over me, cold and sharp. I leaned against the wall, closing my eyes, trying to force the churning feeling down. It wasn't sadness, exactly. It was a profound sense of being cheated, a deep-seated feeling that everything good about that time was now being commodified, packaged, and sold off to strangers. When I opened my eyes, the light seemed too bright, exposing the hollowness inside. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent street, the air thick with a sourness that settled deep in my throat. I let out a low, involuntary sound—a sound devoid of warmth—and turned away, unwilling to look at the ghost of what used to be.

[story 3]

It was late, and the blue light of the monitor cast harsh shadows across my tired features. The listing for Elm Street had appeared amidst the late-night scrolling, an unexpected jolt of unwelcome memory. A slow, deliberate movement took over me; I picked up a cold glass of water and drank it in one go, the liquid doing little to cool the internal furnace raging beneath my ribs. I reread the description, the words mocking the simplicity of my past. Each sentence felt like a deliberate slight, an acknowledgment that the place I’d always felt belonged to me was now just inventory. A sharp, acidic taste rose in my mouth. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing in the small room. I didn't bother to delete the page. Instead, I just sat there, rigidly still, letting the simmering frustration boil over into a restless energy that made my limbs feel heavy and uncooperative.
